500cc – {{MGP|1956}}, {{MGP|1958}}, {{MGP|1959}}, {{MGP|1960}} | GP Race Starts = 68 | GP Race Wins = 38 | GP Podiums = 45 | GP Total Points = 350 | GP Poles = N/A | GP Fastest laps = 34 | GP First win = [[1955 Ulster Grand Prix|1955 250cc Ulster Grand Prix]] | GP Last win = [[1960 Nations motorcycle Grand Prix|1960 500cc Nations Grand Prix]] | GP First race = [[1952 Ulster Grand Prix|1952 500cc Ulster Grand Prix]] | GP Last race = [[1960 Nations motorcycle Grand Prix|1960 500cc Nations Grand Prix]] }} | module2 = {{Infobox F1 driver | embed = yes | nationality = {{flagicon|GBR}} [[Formula One drivers from the United Kingdom|British]] | years = {{F1|1960}}–{{F1|1972}} | teams = [[Team Lotus|Lotus]], [[Reg Parnell Racing|Parnell]], [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]], [[Cooper Car Company|Cooper]], [[Honda in Formula One|Honda]], [[BRM]], [[Surtees Racing Organisation|Surtees]] | races = 113 (111 starts) | championships = 1 ({{F1|1964}}) | wins = 6 | podiums = 24 | points = 180 | poles = 8 | fastest_laps = 10 | first_race = [[1960 Monaco Grand Prix]] | first_win = [[1963 German Grand Prix]] | last_win = [[1967 Italian Grand Prix]] | last_race = [[1972 Italian Grand Prix]] }} | module3 = {{Infobox Le Mans driver | embed = yes | years = {{24hLM|1963}}–{{24hLM|1965}}, {{24hLM|1967}} | teams = [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]], [[Lola Cars|Lola]] | best_finish = 3rd ({{24hLM|1964}}) | class_wins = 0 }} }} '''John Norman Surtees''' (11 February 1934 – 10 March 2017) was a British [[racing driver]] and [[motorcycle road racing|motorcycle road racer]] who competed in [[Grand Prix motorcycle racing]] from {{MGP|1952}} to {{MGP|1960}}, and [[Formula One]] from {{F1|1960}} to {{F1|1972}}. Surtees was a seven-time [[List of Grand Prix motorcycle racing World Riders' Champions|Grand Prix motorcycle World Champion]], with four titles in the premier [[List of 500cc/MotoGP World Riders' Champions|500cc class]] with [[MV Agusta]]. Surtees won the [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship]] in {{F1|1964}} with [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]], and remains the only driver to win World Championships on both [[motorcycle racing|two-]] and [[auto racing|four-wheels]]; he won 38 [[List of Grand Prix motorcycle racing winners|motorcycle Grands Prix]] and six [[Formula One Grands Prix]]. On his way to become a seven-time Grand Prix motorcycle World Champion, Surtees won his first title in 1956, and followed with three consecutive doubles between 1958 and 1960, winning six World Championships in both the 500 and 350cc classes. Surtees then made the move to the pinnacle of four-wheeled motorsport, the Formula One World Championship, and in 1964 made motor racing history by becoming the Formula One World Champion. He founded the [[Surtees Racing Organisation]] team that competed as a constructor in Formula One, [[Formula 2]] and [[Formula 5000]] from 1970 to 1978. He was also the ambassador of the [[Racing Steps Foundation]]. ==Motorcycle racing career== Surtees was the son of a south-London motorcycle dealer.{{Citation |first1=Dennis|last1=Noyes|year=1999|first2= Michael|last2=Scott|title=Motocourse: 50 Years of Moto Grand Prix |publisher =Hazleton Publishing Ltd|isbn=1-874557-83-7}} His father Jack Surtees was an accomplished grasstrack competitor and in 1948 was the South Eastern Centre Sidecar Champion.{{Cite web|url=https://lyddenhill.co.uk/history/|title=History {{!}} Lydden Hill|website=lyddenhill.co.uk|access-date=2019-01-24}} He had his first professional outing, which they won, in the sidecar of his father's [[Vincent Motorcycle|Vincent]] at the age of 14. However, when race officials discovered Surtees's age, they were disqualified. He entered his first race at 15 in a [[grasstrack]] competition. In 1950, at the age of 16, he went to work for the Vincent factory as an apprentice.{{cite web |url=https://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/129/ |title=Formula 1 Hall of Fame |publisher=formula1.com |access-date=7 October 2011 }} He first gained prominence in 1951 when he gave [[Norton (motorcycle)|Norton]] star [[Geoff Duke]] a strong challenge in an [[AutoCycle Union|ACU]] race at the [[Thruxton Circuit]]. In [[1955 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1955]], Norton race chief Joe Craig gave Surtees his first factory-sponsored ride aboard the Nortons. He finished the year by beating reigning world champion Duke at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]] and then at [[Brands Hatch]]. However, with Norton in financial trouble and uncertain about their racing plans, Surtees accepted an offer to race for the [[MV Agusta]] factory racing team, where he soon earned the nickname ''figlio del vento'' (son of the wind).{{cite journal|last=Smith|first=Robert|title=Last of the Breed: MV Agusta 850SS|journal=Motorcycle Classics|date=January–February 2013|volume=8|issue=3|url=https://www.motorcycleclassics.com/classic-italian-motorcycles/classic-agusta-motorcycles/mv-agusta-850ss-zm0z13jfzbea.aspx|access-date=31 January 2013}} [[File:500cc John Surtees (voor zijn val), Bestanddeelnr 911-3657.jpg|thumb|left|Surtees in action during the 1960 500cc Dutch TT.]] In [[1956 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1956]], Surtees won the [[List of Grand Prix motorcycle racing World champions by year|500 cc world championship]],{{cite web |url=http://www.motogp.com/en/riders/profiles/John+Surtees |title=John Surtees career statistics at MotoGP.com |publisher=motogp.com |access-date=7 October 2011 |archive-date=5 September 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120905234554/http://www.motogp.com/en/riders/John%2BSurtees |url-status=dead }} MV Agusta's first in the senior class. In this Surtees was assisted by the [[Fédération Internationale de Motocyclisme|FIM's]] decision to ban the defending champion, Geoff Duke, for six months because of his support for a riders' strike for more starting money.{{cite news |title=Geoff Duke Must Finish Six Months' Suspension |work=The Bulletin |page=8 |date=18 August 1956 |access-date=7 October 2011 |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=KKxBAAAAIBAJ&pg=4656,1403324&dq=geoff+duke&hl=en}} In the [[1957 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1957]] season, the MV Agustas were no match for the [[Gilera]]s and Surtees battled to a third-place finish aboard a 1957 MV Agusta 500 Quattro.{{cite web|url=https://www.motorcycleclassics.com/motorcycle-reviews/2007-07-01/mv-agusta-500-guattro-racer-test.aspx|title=1957 MV Agusta 500 Quattro|publisher=Motorcycle Classics|access-date=12 August 2009|date=July–August 2007|author=Alan Cathcart}} When [[Gilera]] and [[Moto Guzzi]] withdrew from Grand Prix racing at the end of [[1957 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1957]], Surtees and MV Agusta went on to dominate the competition in the two larger displacement classes. In [[1958 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1958]], [[1959 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1959]] and [[1960 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season|1960]], he won 32 out of 39 races and became the first man to win the [[Senior TT]] at the [[Isle of Man TT]] three years in succession.{{cite web |url=http://www.iomtt.com/TT-Database/Events/Races.aspx?meet_code=ALL&ride_id=1641 |title=John Surtees Isle of Man TT results at iomtt.com |publisher=iomtt.com |access-date=7 October 2011 }} ==Auto racing career== [[File:1965-07-30 Surtees u. Forghieri.JPG|thumb|left|Surtees (left) and [[Mauro Forghieri]] in 1965]] [[File:1965-05-23 07b John Surtees, Ferrari 330P2.jpg|thumb|right|Surtees at the 1965 [[1000 km Nürburgring]]]] [[File:Surtees at 1968 Dutch Grand Prix.jpg|thumb|left|Surtees and [[Yoshio Nakamura]] at the [[1968 Dutch Grand Prix]]]] [[File:Surtees at 1969 Dutch Grand Prix.jpg|thumb|right|Surtees at the [[1969 Dutch Grand Prix]]]] [[File:SurteesTS7BarryBoor.jpg|thumb|left|Surtees at the wheel of the [[Surtees TS7]]]] While still racing motorcycles full-time, Surtees performed a test drive in Aston Martin's [[Aston Martin DBR1|DBR1]] sports car in front of team manager Reg Parnell. He however continued on two wheels and did not enter car racing until the following year. In [[1960 Formula One season|1960]], at the age of 26, Surtees switched from motorcycles to cars full-time, making his Formula 1 debut racing in the [[1960 BRDC International Trophy]][https://www.motorsport.com/f1/news/obituary-john-surtees-1934-2017-881549/ Adam Cooper, Obituary: John Surtees, 1934–2017, www.motorsport.com] Retrieved 12 March 2017 at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]] for [[Team Lotus]].[http://www.formula2.net/F160_6.htm XII B.R.D.C. Daily Express International Trophy 1960, www.formula2.net] Retrieved 12 March 2017 He made an immediate impact with a second-place finish in only his second Formula One World Championship race, at the [[1960 British Grand Prix]], and a pole position at his third, the [[1960 Portuguese Grand Prix]]. After spending the [[1961 Formula One season|1961]] season with the Yeoman Credit Racing Team driving a Cooper T53 "Lowline" managed by Reg Parnell and the [[1962 Formula One season|1962]] season with the Bowmaker Racing Team, still managed by Reg Parnell but now in the V8 Lola Mk4, he moved to [[Scuderia Ferrari]] in 1963 and won the World Championship for the Italian team in [[1964 Formula One season|1964]].{{cite web |url=http://www.4mula1.ro/history/driver/John_Surtees |title=John Surtees Formula One statistics |publisher=4mula1.ro |access-date=7 October 2011 |archive-date=27 September 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110927235959/http://www.4mula1.ro/history/driver/John_Surtees |url-status=dead }} On 25 September 1965, Surtees had a life-threatening accident at the [[Mosport Park]] Circuit (Ontario, Canada) while practising in a [[Lola T70]] sports racing car. A front upright casting had broken. A.J. Baime in his book ''Go Like Hell'' says Surtees came out of the crash with one side of his body four inches shorter than the other.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4ddp0G3z-y0C |title=Go Like Hell: Ford, Ferrari and their Battle for Speed and Glory at Le Mans |last=Baime |first=A. J. |publisher=Random House |year=2011 |isbn=9781446497463 |page=262}} Doctors set most of the breaks nonsurgically, in part by physically stretching his shattered body until the right-left discrepancy was under an inch – and there it stayed. The [[1966 Formula One season|1966 season]] saw the introduction of new, larger 3-litre engines to Formula One.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kW5eCAAAQBAJ&pg=PT143 |title=Collection Editions: Ferrari in Formula One |last=Buckland |first=Damien |publisher=Lulu Press, Inc |year=2015 |isbn=9781326174880 |page=143}} Surtees's debut with Ferrari's new F1 car was at the [[1966 BRDC International Trophy]] at Silverstone, where he qualified and finished a close second behind [[Jack Brabham]]'s 3-litre [[Brabham BT19]].{{Cite web |url=https://drivetribe.com/p/LH6KT4zPQkWlMb-RghZ9aQ?iid=FN0CDG18S46vt88tmzXYng |title=Of His Own Construction – 1966 Repco Brabham BT19 |last=Masin |first=Michael |website=drivetribe.com |access-date=10 March 2017 |archive-date=12 March 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312055841/https://drivetribe.com/p/LH6KT4zPQkWlMb-RghZ9aQ?iid=FN0CDG18S46vt88tmzXYng |url-status=dead }} A few weeks later, Surtees led the [[Monaco Grand Prix]], pulling away from [[Jackie Stewart]]'s 2-litre [[BRM]] on the straights, before the engine failed. A fortnight later Surtees survived the first lap rainstorm which eliminated half the field and won the [[1966 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgian Grand Prix]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cPAhDg87jz4C&pg=PA137 |title=Real Racers |last=Codling |first=Stuart |year=2011 |publisher=MBI Publishing Company |isbn=9781610597395 |page=137}} Due to perennial strikes in Italy, Ferrari could afford to enter only two cars (Ferrari P3s) for the [[1966 24 Hours of Le Mans]] instead of its usual entry of three prototypes. Uncertainty and confusion surrounds subsequent events and their consequences, and a number of different explanations have been offered in the decades since. The narrative explained by Ferrari at the time states that under Le Mans rules in 1966 each car was allowed only two drivers. Surtees was omitted from the driver line-up with one works Ferrari to be driven by Mike Parkes and Ludovico Scarfiotti, and the other by Jean Guichet and Lorenzo Bandini. When Surtees questioned Ferrari team manager Eugenio Dragoni as to why, as the Ferrari team leader, he would not be allowed to compete, Dragoni told Surtees that he did not feel that he was fully fit to drive in a 24-hour endurance race because of the injuries he had sustained in late 1965. However, Surtees himself described things somewhat differently. In his recollection, when the pairings were announced he was to drive alongside Scarfiotti. As the faster driver of the two, Surtees argued that he should take the first stint and "try to break" the Ford opposition by driving "flat out from the start".{{Cite magazine|title= Lunch with... John Surtees |author= Taylor, Simon |date= October 2015 |url= https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-2015/68/lunch-john-surtees |magazine= [[Motor Sport (magazine)|Motor Sport]] |volume= 91 |issue= 10 |pages= 68–76 |access-date= 18 June 2017 }} Dragoni denied Surtees's request and insisted that Scarfiotti take the start, supposedly to please [[Fiat S.p.A.|Fiat]] chairman [[Gianni Agnelli]], Scarfiotti's uncle, who was in attendance as a spectator. Either way, the decision and subsequent lack of support from Enzo Ferrari were deeply upsetting to Surtees and he immediately quit the team.{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/formula1/35126201 |title=John Surtees: Former F1 world champion was a 'towering figure' |last=Benson |first=Andrew |access-date=11 March 2017 |publisher=BBC |date=10 March 2017}} This decision likely cost both Ferrari and Surtees the Formula 1 Championship in 1966. Ferrari finished second to Brabham-Repco in the Constructors' Championship and Surtees finished second to Jack Brabham in the Drivers' Championship.''Go Like Hell: Ford, Ferrari, and Their Battle for Speed and Glory at Le Mans''. by A.J.Baime Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2009. {{ISBN|978-0-618-82219-5}} Surtees finished the season driving for the [[Cooper Car Company|Cooper]]-[[Maserati in motorsport|Maserati]] team, winning the last race of the season.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mXRZ9OvEsWgC&pg=PA35 |title=Complete Encyclopedia of Formula One: Presenting the Drivers, Cars, Circuits and Every |publisher=Parragon Publishing India |isbn=9781445405353 |page=35}} Surtees competed with a T70 in the inaugural [[1966 Can-Am season]],{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UnNUiPWfnvQC&pg=PT21 |title=Aston Martin: Power, Beauty and Soul |last=Dowsey |first=David |publisher=Images Publishing |year=2010 |isbn=9781864704242 |edition=illustrated |page=21}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zp3ZAAAAMAAJ |title=The encyclopaedia of motor racing |last=Davey |first=Keith |publisher=D. McKay Co. |year=1969 |page=182|isbn=9780709107934 }} winning three races of six to become champion{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lifwAAAAMAAJ |title=George Eaton: Five Minutes to Green: The Anatomy of a Young Canadian Auto Racer's First Season as a Professional Driver in Competitive Cars |last=Orr |first=Frank |publisher=Longman Canada |year=1970 |pages=21}} over other winners [[Dan Gurney]] (Lola), [[Mark Donohue]] (Lola) and [[Phil Hill]] (Chaparral) as well as the likes of [[Bruce McLaren]] and [[Chris Amon]] (both in McLarens).{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dJjsAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA46 |title=Lola T70: The Racing History and Individual Chassis Record |last=Starkey |first=John |publisher=Veloce Publishing Ltd |year=2002 |isbn=9781903706138 |edition=illustrated |page=46}} In December 1966, Surtees signed for [[Honda in Formula One|Honda]].{{cite journal|date=1 December 1966|title=Surtees settles for Honda|journal=Auto News|publisher=Motor Cycle News Ltd|location=Peterborough|issue=31}} After a promising third place in the first race in [[1967 South African Grand Prix|South Africa]], the [[Honda RA273]] hit a series of mechanical problems. The car was replaced by the [[Honda RA300]] for the [[1967 Italian Grand Prix|Italian Grand Prix]], where Surtees [[Drafting (aerodynamics)|slipstreamed]] Jack Brabham to take Honda's second F1 victory by 0.2 seconds. Surtees finished fourth in the 1967 Drivers' Championship. The same year, Surtees drove in the ''Rex Mays 300'' at [[Riverside International Raceway|Riverside]], near Los Angeles, in a [[United States Auto Club]] season-ending road race. This event pitted the best American drivers of the day – normally those who had cut their teeth as professional drivers on oval dirt tracks – against veteran Formula One Grand Prix drivers, including [[Jim Clark]] and Dan Gurney.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Klc7AAAAMAAJ |title=Auto Driver |publisher=Counterpoint |year=1967 |volume=67 |page=123}} In [[1970 Formula One season|1970]], Surtees formed his own race team, the [[Surtees Racing Organisation]], and spent nine seasons competing in [[Formula 5000]], [[Formula 2]] and Formula 1 as a constructor. He retired from competitive driving in [[1972 Formula One season|1972]], the same year the team had their greatest success when [[Mike Hailwood]] won the European Formula 2 Championship.{{cite web |url=http://www.formula2.net/F272_Tables.htm |title=1972 Formula Two results |publisher=formula2.net |access-date=7 October 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170202054842/http://formula2.net/F272_Tables.htm |archive-date=2 February 2017 }} The team was finally disbanded at the end of [[1978 Formula One season|1978]].{{Cite news |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/motor-racing/john-surtees-dead-f1-world-champion-motorbike-aged-83-a7623281.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220613/https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/motor-racing/john-surtees-dead-f1-world-champion-motorbike-aged-83-a7623281.html |archive-date=13 June 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |title=John Surtees dead: Former F1 and motorbike world champion dies, aged 83 |access-date=10 March 2017 |work=The Independent |date=10 March 2017 }} ==After Formula One== [[File:John Surtees at Goodwood Revival 2011 (cropped).jpg|thumb|right|John Surtees in 2011]] For a while in the 1970s Surtees ran a motorcycle shop in West Wickham, Kent, and a Honda car dealership in Edenbridge, Kent.[https://web.archive.org/web/20170316132155/http://www.surreymirror.co.uk/8203-surrey-born-f1-and-world-motorcycle-champion-john-surtees-has-died/story-30195218-detail/story.html Surrey racing legend John Surtees who won F1 and world motorcycle championships has died (Archived from the original)] [[Surrey Advertiser|''Surrey Mirror'']], 10 March 2017 Retrieved 2 December 2017 He continued his involvement in motorcycling, participating in classic events with bikes from his stable of vintage racing machines. He also remained involved in single-seater racing cars and held the position of chairman of [[A1 Team Great Britain]], in the [[A1 Grand Prix]] racing series from 2005 to 2007. His son, [[Henry Surtees]], competed in the FIA Formula 2 Championship, Formula Renault UK Championship and the [[Formula BMW]] UK championship for [[Carlin Motorsport]],{{cite web|url= http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6946783.stm|title= How to become F1 champion|access-date= 22 August 2007|date= 22 August 2007|work = Sarah Holt|publisher = BBC}} before he died while racing in the Formula 2 championship at Brands Hatch on 19 July 2009.{{cite news |title=John Surtees' son Henry killed in Formula Two accident |work=The Telegraph |date=19 July 2009 |access-date=7 October 2011 |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/motorsport/5866783/John-Surtees-son-Henry-killed-in-Formula-Two-accident.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220112/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/motorsport/5866783/John-Surtees-son-Henry-killed-in-Formula-Two-accident.html |archive-date=12 January 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live}}{{cbignore}} In 2010,{{Cite web|url=https://beta.companieshouse.gov.uk/company/07254932|title=HENRY SURTEES FOUNDATION – Overview (free company information from Companies House)|website=beta.companieshouse.gov.uk|language=en|access-date=2018-08-28}} Surtees founded the Henry Surtees Foundation in his son's memory, as a charitable organization to assist victims of accidental brain injuries and to promote safety in driving and motorsport.{{Cite news|url=https://www.driving.co.uk/news/obituary-john-surtees/|title=Obituary: John 2010|title=Henry Surtees Foundation|website=henrysurteesfoundation.com|access-date=2018-08-28}} Surtees was the subject of ''[[This Is Your Life (British TV series)|This Is Your Life]]'' in 1992 when "Legend" in 2003.{{cite web |url=http://www.motogp.com/en/MotoGP+Legends |title=MotoGP Legends |publisher=motogp.com |access-date=7 October 2011 |archive-date=19 January 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170119183854/http://www.motogp.com/en/MotoGP%2BLegends |url-status=dead }} Already a [[Member of the Order of the British Empire]] (MBE), he was appointed an |website=johnsurtees.com |date=23 June 2015 |access-date=10 March 2017 |archive-date=12 March 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312063500/http://www.johnsurtees.com/?p=364 |url-status=dead }}{{Cite web |url=https://www.brookes.ac.uk/about-brookes/honorary-graduates/list-by-year/ |title=Honorary Graduates |website=Oxford Brookes University |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170316133338/https://www.brookes.ac.uk/about-brookes/honorary-graduates/list-by-year/ |archive-date=16 March 2017 }} Surtees was inducted into the [[Motorsports Hall of Fame of America]] in 2024.{{citation needed|date=November 2025}} ==Personal life and death== Surtees married three times, first to Patricia Burke in 1962; the couple divorced in 1979. His second wife was Janis Sheara, whom he married in 1979 and they divorced in 1982. Jane Sparrow was his third wife, whom he married in 1987, and with whom he had three children, including [[Henry Surtees|Henry]]. Henry would later become a racing driver, but was killed at [[Brands Hatch]] in the [[2009 FIA Formula Two Championship]].{{Cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/mar/10/john-surtees-obituary |title=John Surtees obituary |access-date=10 March 2017 |work=The Guardian |date=10 March 2017}} Surtees died of respiratory failure on 10 March 2017 at [[St George's Hospital]] in London, at the age of 83.{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/formula1/39235608 |title=John Surtees: Former F1 world champion dies at 83 |access-date=10 March 2017 |publisher=BBC |date=10 March 2017}}{{Cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/mar/10/john-surtees-formula-one-dies |title=John Surtees, former F1 and motorcycle world champion, dies aged 83 |access-date=10 March 2017 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=10 March 2017}}{{Cite web |title=Motorsport Memorial - John Surtees |url=http://www.motorsportmemorial.org/LWFWIW/focusLWFWIW.php?db=LWF&db2=ms&n=2137 |access-date=22 March 2023 |website=Motorsport Memorial}} He was buried, next to his son Henry, at St Peter and St Paul's Church in [[Lingfield, Surrey]].
